old_claim.note: Legal Systems (Common/Civil Law) is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim.note: Roman-law and civil-law texts, especially the Corpus Juris Civilis, supplied the legal corpus glossators analyzed; this is textual legal context rather than a strict institutional prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Content, Timeline, and Legacy sections identify the Codex, Digest, and Institutes as Justinian's legal corpus and describe their later influence on western legal systems.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes the Corpus Juris Civilis as a late-antique Roman legal compilation whose Codex, Digest, and Institutes became durable legal authorities for later western legal systems.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That pins the legal corpus glossators commented on, while preserving the edge as common textual context because the source does not say a particular civil-law institution was a hard prerequisite for every glossator.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: legal_commentary_glossators keeps legal_systems_common_civil as common legal-text context, with evidence narrowed to Justinianic Roman-law materials.
would_reject_if: The glossator node were broadened to all medieval commentary rather than Roman-law glossators.
The legal_systems_common_civil node were narrowed to English common law only and no longer covered Justinianic civil-law texts.
A stronger source showed glossators did not work from the Corpus Iuris Civilis or related Roman-law materials.
short_reason: Glossators commented on Justinianic Roman-law texts.
